Q1: I tried to keep things organized, but honestly it was a bit chaotic with limited resources at home. I'd usually set aside specific blocks during the week—mostly evenings after work and weekend mornings—to go through video lectures and readings. I'm a visual learner, so I'd create diagrams and color-coded notes to help things stick. With 23 hours a week dedicated to studying, I had to be strategic about what I focused on, even if I couldn't always follow through perfectly.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I aimed for consistency, but when assignments piled up or when I had other responsibilities, everything shifted. Some weeks I'd be really disciplined, logging in every day, and other weeks I'd cram everything into a couple of intense sessions. Being 25 and managing life outside university means flexibility isn't always a choice—it's a necessity.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new content, seeing if there were announcements, or working on the gamified tasks they introduced. Those tasks actually made me want to log in more because there was something satisfying about earning points and seeing progress. Sometimes I'd log in just to see where I stood compared to my own previous performance. When I felt scared about falling behind, that fear also pushed me to check in more frequently.
Q4: Yeah, the gamified elements really helped make logging in feel like a habit rather than a chore. After a few weeks, I found myself checking the LMS almost automatically, especially in the mornings before I started my day. It became part of my routine to see if there were new challenges or if my ranking had changed. That sense of progression, even small wins, kept me coming back consistently.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me—I watched nearly 300 minutes worth because I learn best when I can see concepts visually explained. The discussion forums were also valuable because interacting with peers helped clarify things I was confused about. I posted 19 times and had 18 peer interactions, which doesn't sound like much, but each one felt meaningful. The gamified tasks made even mundane activities feel more engaging and worth completing.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete all the readings, especially the dense text-heavy PDFs. With my visual learning style, walls of text just don't stick as well, and I'd often skim them or skip to the summaries. Some optional quizzes I delayed because they felt low-priority compared to graded assignments. When resources are limited and time is tight, you have to make choices about where to invest your energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to either my grades or my actual understanding of the material. The gamified tasks were great because they gave immediate feedback and a sense of accomplishment. If something felt like busywork with no real payoff, I'd deprioritize it. I also valued activities where I could interact with others because that social element made learning feel less isolating.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure, which was both good and bad. Sometimes the lack of pressure meant I'd put them off indefinitely, even if they might have helped me. Required activities had that urgency that forced me to engage even when I wasn't feeling motivated. The gamified optional tasks were an exception though; they felt rewarding enough that I actually wanted to do them.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials to study offline because my internet isn't always reliable at home. I'd save videos and PDFs, then work through them without being connected. I also used external YouTube videos and free resources to supplement what the course offered. My notes were all handwritten with diagrams and visual maps that I'd study from constantly, but none of that shows up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also discuss concepts with a friend who's in a similar program, and we'd work through difficult topics together over the phone. Sometimes I'd watch a lecture once online, then replay it mentally while commuting or doing chores. A lot of my learning happened in my head, not on a screen.
Q11: Yes, constantly. With my internet situation, I had to be practical about it. I'd download lecture videos and watch them multiple times offline, pausing to take detailed visual notes. PDFs and slides got saved to my phone so I could review them during breaks at work. One download might turn into several hours of offline study that the system would never capture.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. I felt scared about whether I could keep up, and that anxiety sometimes made it hard to even start working. When I was juggling multiple deadlines and life responsibilities, the course felt overwhelming. My motivation wasn't always high, so pushing through those periods took real effort and resilience.
Q13: Definitely. Fatigue was a big one—after long days, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Financial stress and limited resources meant I sometimes had to prioritize work over studying. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections, which probably hurt my assignment completion rate. When competing responsibilities hit, the LMS was often the first thing to suffer.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be logged in, maybe even watching a video, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be worried about bills or just mentally exhausted, and the content wouldn't register at all. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Being physically present on the platform didn't always mean I was actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was fear—I'd feel scared about not doing well, so I'd avoid starting. Low motivation made it easy to justify putting things off until later. Sometimes the task just felt too big, and breaking it down felt overwhelming. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with its own problems.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly my main driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, logging in frequently and trying to catch up. Without deadlines, I probably would have spread things out more evenly, but that's not how my brain works. The gamified tasks helped because they created mini-deadlines that kept me engaged between the big ones. Deadlines created stress but also structure.
Q17: Feedback had a real impact on me. When I got positive feedback or saw improvement in my scores, it motivated me to keep going. Negative feedback sometimes made me anxious and hesitant to engage with similar tasks. The immediate feedback from gamified activities was especially helpful because I didn't have to wait to know if I was on the right track. I wished there was more personalized feedback on assignments though.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to follow, which I needed given my tendency to procrastinate. Having modules released on a schedule helped me plan my study time around them. The gamified elements added a layer of structure that made engagement feel more rewarding. However, when the structure was too rigid or didn't account for life happening, it became stressful rather than helpful.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they turned studying into something almost enjoyable. Visual content like videos and diagrams aligned well with how I learn, so that helped too. What made it harder was when there were too many text-heavy readings without visual aids. Also, when the platform was slow or my internet struggled, it created friction that discouraged me from staying engaged.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying hard but struggling to produce quality work. They might be watching videos repeatedly, reading discussions, trying to understand, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. Fear of failure can make you engage with content without ever committing to submitting something. It could also be that life circumstances prevent them from completing work even when they're putting in effort.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show in the LMS, but it contributed to my exam performance. Someone might have strong foundational knowledge or learn efficiently from fewer interactions with the platform. It's also possible they're using external resources that work better for their learning style. The LMS only captures one slice of how learning actually happens.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how long you spend on videos, whether you're participating in discussions. They're good at tracking frequency and duration of engagement. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and whether anything is actually being learned. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or the depth of understanding someone has developed.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine effort into understanding it. It's not just logging in or completing tasks—it's being mentally present and curious. For me, engagement includes those moments when I'm drawing diagrams at 2 AM trying to connect concepts, or when I'm discussing ideas with peers. It's about investment, not just activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my fear and anxiety about the course, or how much resilience it took to keep going despite low motivation. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying from handwritten notes or the conversations with friends that helped me understand difficult concepts. My financial constraints and how they affected my study patterns are invisible to the system. The emotional journey of getting through this course—feeling scared, pushing through, and ultimately passing—that's completely hidden from any log.
